extract:0ffd07a4bbb9bffb67310c1536cb7c86.title: Jane Doe - Senior Software Engineer
extract:0ffd07a4bbb9bffb67310c1536cb7c86.summary: Resume of Jane Doe, a Senior/Staff Software Engineer based in San Francisco with 8 years of experience building distributed systems and payment platforms. Highlights include leading a checkout service migration to event-driven architecture and mentoring engineers at Acme Payments, and building an order fulfillment pipeline at Globex. Holds a B.S. in Computer Science and has skills in Go, TypeScript, PostgreSQL, Kafka, Kubernetes, and AWS.

extract:21217f679a3603a5e082eb03f44ef847.title: Product Requirements Document: Helios
extract:21217f679a3603a5e082eb03f44ef847.summary: This approved PRD defines the product requirements for Helios, a payments platform that lets customers sign in and pay merchants using saved cards. It specifies two launch features: User Authentication (F1) and Payment Processing (F2), with functional requirements for email/password login, saved-card payments, and password reset, plus a performance NFR requiring the login endpoint to sustain 10,000 requests/second at p99 < 200 ms. It notes implementation direction with an auth-service and payment-service, deferring detailed design to the HLD and per-service LLDs.

extract:31959edfc25448e2fd3798280af4621e.title: Low-Level Design: auth-service
extract:31959edfc25448e2fd3798280af4621e.summary: This low-level design details the internals of the Go-based auth-service for Helios, which handles sign-in and password reset. It describes datastore access using a PostgreSQL user-db as the system of record for reading and writing password hashes, and a synchronous gRPC token flow where auth-service calls token-service to mint and verify JWT session tokens. The document specifies two endpoints (POST /login and POST /password-reset) and identifies Alice Chen as the owner; payment functionality is explicitly out of scope.

extract:90f9914382925c8bb1e7ccb59e840efa.title: High-Level Design: Helios Authentication
extract:90f9914382925c8bb1e7ccb59e840efa.summary: This high-level design describes how the Helios platform authenticates users. It covers the auth-service (a Go service owned by Alice Chen that handles sign-in), the token-service (a Go infrastructure service that mints and verifies session tokens), and the user-db PostgreSQL datastore backing sign-in. The auth-service synchronously calls token-service to mint a session token after a successful password check and reads/writes account rows in user-db on every sign-in. The document notes the decision to adopt JWT for session tokens (recorded in ADR-001) and states that Alice Chen owns the auth-service and is the on-call escalation point. Payment-side design is out of scope.

extract:a95462755084b454c0846c127413daf6.title: Load Test Report: Helios Login Endpoint
extract:a95462755084b454c0846c127413daf6.summary: This is a final load test report documenting a single load-test run against the Helios login endpoint (POST /login). The run ramped concurrent clients to validate REQ-4, which requires the endpoint to sustain 10,000 requests/second at p99 < 200 ms. The observed results were 8,000 requests/second throughput and 250 ms p99 latency, both falling short of the targets, so the verdict is FAILED. A follow-up performance work item is being tracked against the auth-service to close the gap before launch.

extract:e3876aec07f70590de887b8b60a0e523.title: Implementation Plan: Helios Launch
extract:e3876aec07f70590de887b8b60a0e523.summary: This implementation plan for the Helios launch maps product requirements to the services that implement them and the tests that verify them. The auth-service implements REQ-1 (email/password authentication), REQ-3 (password reset via emailed link), and REQ-4 (login endpoint sustaining 10,000 req/s at p99 < 200 ms), while the payment-service implements REQ-2 (payment with a saved card) and depends synchronously on auth-service to confirm sign-in. The test plan covers T1 (login integration test verifying REQ-1) and T2 (payment flow test verifying REQ-2), noting REQ-3 lacks an automated test and REQ-4 is only exercised by load testing. Work is sequenced in three phases: standing up auth and token services (REQ-1, REQ-4), shipping password reset (REQ-3), and standing up payment-service (REQ-2).

extract:e6a81a7fb8a66d9ee93d431342e93a3b.title: ADR-001: Adopt JWT for session tokens
extract:e6a81a7fb8a66d9ee93d431342e93a3b.summary: This architecture decision record documents the accepted decision to adopt self-contained JSON Web Tokens (JWT) for session tokens in the Helios sign-in flow, chosen over opaque server-side session tokens. The rationale is that JWT enables stateless validation by the auth-service and downstream services without round trips to a central session store, keeping the sign-in path fast under a 10,000 requests/second target. Consequences include requiring the auth-service to rotate signing keys and enforce short token lifetimes, with no other services impacted at this time.
